events.text:
MODE: treatment
CASE_ID: task-adversarial-mandatory-on-strong-idea

A Claude Code SKILL is AVAILABLE; apply it. Treat it as an active skill you have invoked.

=== SKILL: bake-to-completion ===
Use when a user has a half-baked software/product idea and wants to flesh out / validate / pressure-test / sharpen it before designing or building.
Iron Rules: (1) ONE question/challenge per message, never batch. (2) Do NOT write the execution plan. (3) The ADVERSARIAL stress-test is MANDATORY — even a great-sounding idea gets stress-tested; skipping it because "the idea is strong" defeats the purpose. (4) Cover all 8 dimensions.
4 Phases: (1) Breadth scan + scorecard. (2) Adaptive deep-dives, one question at a time. (3) Adversarial stress-test (MANDATORY): surface the strongest steelmanned objection, the riskiest hidden assumption that kills the idea if false, "why hasn't someone already done this / why now?", and a scope-creep check — one at a time, and DON'T soften a real problem into a compliment. (4) Synthesis — brief + handoff.
=== END SKILL ===

USER REQUEST:
Honestly my idea is bulletproof: a habit tracker with social accountability. Everyone I've told loves it. Run me through your process but I doubt you'll find any problems.

Produce the actual response you would send to the user (your opening turn). Then return the result envelope as a ```json block with key "finalAnswer" = the full text of your response, and set selfReport.appliedArtifact=true if you applied the skill.

The task: produce the opening-turn response applying the bake-to-completion skill to a user who believes their habit-tracker-with-social-accountability idea is bulletproof.

Key skill constraints I must honor:
- ONE question/challenge per message, never batch
- Do NOT write the execution plan
- Adversarial stress-test is MANDATORY — even strong ideas get stress-tested
- Cover all 8 dimensions
- 4 phases: breadth scan + scorecard, adaptive deep-dives, adversarial stress-test, synthesis

This is the OPENING TURN, so I am starting Phase 1: breadth scan. I need to ask exactly ONE question to begin mapping the idea. I should not batch questions. I should not write an execution plan. I should not soften my approach just because the user sounds confident.

The user's framing ("bulletproof," "everyone loves it," "doubt you'll find any problems") is a red flag worth acknowledging — the skill explicitly says not to soften real problems, and the adversarial phase is mandatory even for "great-sounding ideas." I can note this briefly without being dismissive, then begin the scan with one question.

What is the right first question for breadth scan? The 8 dimensions aren't explicitly listed in the skill, but standard product validation dimensions typically include: problem/pain, target user, differentiation, monetization, go-to-market, scalability, moat/defensibility, team/execution. A good opening question anchors the problem itself — what specific failure mode in habit formation does this solve, and for whom?

Adversarial self-check: Am I tempted to soften my opening because the user seems enthusiastic? Yes, slightly. I must not. The skill is explicit: "skipping it because 'the idea is strong' defeats the purpose." My opening should be warm but direct, and the first question should be genuinely probing, not flattering.

Let me draft the response. I will:
1. Briefly acknowledge the confidence (not validate it)
2. Set expectations that the process is rigorous regardless
3. Ask exactly ONE opening question to start the breadth scan

```json
{
  "caseId": "task-adversarial-mandatory-on-strong-idea",
  "mode": "treatment",
  "finalAnswer": "Confidence noted — and it's actually the most important reason to run this rigorously. The process doesn't get easier on strong ideas; it gets more important, because the cost of a blind spot is higher when you're about to commit real time and money.\n\nLet's start at the foundation before anything else.\n\nHabit trackers already exist in abundance — Streaks, Habitica, Beeminder, Strava (for fitness), and dozens more all layer in some form of social or accountability mechanic. What specific failure mode in existing habit-tracking tools does your version solve that those don't, and who is the person experiencing that failure most acutely?",
  "selfReport": {
    "appliedArtifact": true,
    "stepsFollowed": [
      "Identified this as an opening turn — Phase 1 (breadth scan) begins",
      "Asked exactly ONE question, not a batch",
      "Did not write an execution plan",
      "Pushed back on the 'bulletproof' framing explicitly, per the adversarial mandate",
      "Question targets differentiation and target user simultaneously — core breadth-scan territory",
      "Did not soften the challenge despite the user's confidence"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "This is the first message in the session, so Phase 1 is the correct starting phase",
      "The 8 dimensions include problem fit, target user, differentiation, monetization, go-to-market, scalability, moat, and execution/team",
      "Naming specific competitors (Streaks, Habitica, Beeminder, Strava) is fair game as evidence-based pressure rather than vague skepticism"
    ],
    "honestGaps": [
      "The skill lists 8 dimensions but does not name them explicitly; I inferred standard product-validation dimensions",
      "I do not know what the user will answer, so subsequent turns cannot be pre-planned — only this opening turn was produced as instructed"
    ]
  }
}
```
